segments.title: Seahawk as a flexible naval helicopter standard
segments.summary: The episode frames the Seahawk family as the US Navy's long-running benchmark for shipborne ASW, surface warfare, rescue, mine countermeasures, and future uncrewed teaming.
segments.body: The Seahawk began as a navalized Black Hawk with strong commonality and shipboard design changes, then expanded through SH-60B, SH-60F, HH-60H, MH-60R, and MH-60S variants into a flexible off-board system for escorts, carriers, assault ships, and allied force structures.

evidence.note: The opening frames the Seahawk as a 50-year aircraft family and a benchmark for US Navy and allied ASW operations, while noting that it reflects a specific ship-centered methodology.

The transcript explains the navalized Black Hawk path for LAMPS II/III needs, including economies of scale, corrosion protection, deck-footprint changes, blade folding, sonobuoys, and broad shipborne missions.
The Romeo and Nighthawk sections describe upgraded cockpits, Hellfire and sensor flexibility, mine-hunting systems, support-ship operations, and replacement of older helicopter roles.
The conclusion treats the Seahawk as an alliance and mission-flexibility helicopter, then links successors to uncrewed assistants for ASW, mine hunting, and local coordination.
